findings.message: spark.sql.shuffle.partitions is not set — default of 200 is rarely correct
findings.explanation: ``spark.sql.shuffle.partitions`` controls the number of partitions produced by wide transformations (joins, aggregations, window functions). The default of 200 is chosen for a 'medium' dataset on a small cluster and is almost always wrong: too high for small datasets (hundreds of tiny files, shuffle overhead dominates) or too low for large datasets (GBs of data per partition, spill to disk, OOM). The rule of thumb is 2–3 partitions per CPU core available for the stage, or target 128–256 MB per partition. With AQE enabled (Spark 3+), the coalesce-partitions feature can automatically merge small post-shuffle partitions, reducing the impact of over-partitioning.
findings.recommendation: Set ``spark.sql.shuffle.partitions`` based on your cluster size. A starting formula: ``(total_executor_cores × 2) or (dataset_size_gb × 8)``. With AQE enabled, prefer a slightly higher value and let AQE coalesce down.
findings.estimated_impact: Spill to disk on large datasets; small-file explosion on small datasets

findings.message: spark.sql.shuffle.partitions not set — Spark uses the default of 200
findings.explanation:
The default of 200 shuffle partitions was chosen for a small benchmark cluster circa 2014.  It is almost never correct:

• **Too high for small data**: hundreds of tasks each reading a few KB produce   massive scheduler overhead and tiny output files that slow downstream readers.
• **Too low for large data**: partitions grow to multiple GBs, spilling to disk   and overwhelming executor memory.

The correct value depends on cluster size and data volume.  A practical starting point: ``2–3 × (total executor cores)`` or aim for 128–256 MB per post-shuffle partition.  With AQE enabled (``spark.sql.adaptive.enabled=true``) in Spark 3+, you can over-partition slightly and rely on AQE's coalesce-partitions to merge empty/small partitions automatically.
findings.recommendation: Set ``spark.sql.shuffle.partitions`` explicitly. Rule of thumb: ``max(total_executor_cores * 2, dataset_size_gb * 8)``. Enable AQE to auto-tune at runtime: ``spark.sql.adaptive.enabled = true``.
findings.estimated_impact: Spill to disk on large datasets; task-scheduler overhead on small datasets

findings.message: spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabled = false disables automatic skew partition splitting
findings.explanation:
Spark 3.0+ Adaptive Query Execution (AQE) includes an automatic skew join optimisation that detects partitions larger than ``skewedPartitionThresholdInBytes × skewedPartitionFactor`` and automatically splits them across multiple tasks.  This is the lowest-effort fix for many skew problems.

Disabling AQE entirely (``spark.sql.adaptive.enabled = false``) or the skew join feature specifically (``spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabled = false``) removes this safety net, turning every skewed join into a long-running straggler that blocks the stage.

The feature was disabled in early Spark 3.0 betas due to occasional incorrect plan rewrites, but has been stable since Spark 3.1.  There is almost never a valid reason to disable it in Spark 3.1+.
findings.recommendation: Remove the ``false`` override or set both: ``spark.sql.adaptive.enabled = true`` and ``spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabled = true``.
findings.estimated_impact: Skewed partitions are not split; one task processes majority of data

findings.message: spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabled = false while skew-prone joins are present — re-enable to auto-split skewed partitions
findings.explanation:
AQE's skew join optimisation automatically detects shuffle partitions that are disproportionately large compared to the median and splits them across multiple tasks.  This is the lowest-effort fix for join-induced data skew.

This finding fires when ``spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabled = false`` is set in the same file that contains joins on columns that commonly carry power-law or low-cardinality distributions (``user_id``, ``product_id``, ``status``, ``is_active`` …).  These joins are the primary beneficiaries of AQE skew handling, so disabling the feature here is particularly impactful.

Re-enabling skew join handling (the default) allows Spark to automatically split the dominant partitions without any code changes.
findings.recommendation: Remove the ``false`` override: ``spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabled = true`` (or remove the config entry entirely).
findings.after_code:
# Remove the skewJoin.enabled = false line
df.join(events, 'user_id')  # AQE now auto-splits skewed partitions
findings.estimated_impact: Skewed partitions on joins not auto-split; straggler tasks persist

findings.message: join() without broadcast() hint — wrap small DataFrames in broadcast()
findings.explanation:
When one side of a join is significantly smaller than the other (a typical fact-dimension or fact-lookup pattern), wrapping it in ``broadcast()`` tells Spark to send the small table to every executor as a hash map, eliminating the shuffle entirely.

Without a broadcast hint, Spark falls back to a sort-merge join, which shuffles **both** DataFrames across the network — often orders of magnitude more I/O.

Spark's ``spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold`` auto-broadcasts tables below a size limit (default 10 MB).  For tables slightly above the threshold, or when auto-broadcast is disabled, explicit hints are the only way to trigger a broadcast.
findings.recommendation: Wrap the smaller DataFrame in ``broadcast()``: ``df.join(broadcast(small_df), 'id')``. For AQE-managed queries in Spark 3+, also set ``spark.sql.adaptive.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold`` to a reasonable value.
findings.estimated_impact: Eliminates shuffle on both sides; 10–100× faster for small lookups

findings.message: File has 2 join(s) but 'spark.sql.cbo.enabled' is not 'true' — enable CBO for cost-based join strategy selection
findings.explanation:
Spark's Cost-Based Optimiser (CBO) uses table and column statistics — collected via ``ANALYZE TABLE`` — to estimate the cardinality and size of intermediate results.  With CBO enabled, Catalyst can:

• Choose the correct join strategy (broadcast vs sort-merge vs shuffle-hash).
• Re-order joins to process smaller tables first.
• Push down filters based on column statistics (min/max ranges).

Without CBO, Catalyst falls back to heuristic rules that assume worst-case sizes, often choosing sort-merge joins where a broadcast join would be far cheaper.  On queries with 2+ joins the difference can be 5–20× in plan quality.  Enable CBO globally in the session config, then collect statistics with ``ANALYZE TABLE t COMPUTE STATISTICS FOR ALL COLUMNS``.
findings.recommendation: Enable CBO: spark.conf.set('spark.sql.cbo.enabled', 'true'). Then collect table statistics: ANALYZE TABLE t COMPUTE STATISTICS.
findings.after_code:
spark = (
    SparkSession.builder
    .config('spark.sql.cbo.enabled', 'true')
    .config('spark.sql.cbo.joinReorder.enabled', 'true')
    .getOrCreate()
)
spark.sql('ANALYZE TABLE a COMPUTE STATISTICS FOR ALL COLUMNS')
result = a.join(b, 'id').join(c, 'key')
findings.estimated_impact: Sub-optimal join strategies; 5–20× slower plans for multi-join queries

findings.message: 4 shuffle operations in sequence without caching: join, groupBy, orderBy, join
findings.explanation:
Each wide transformation (``join``, ``groupBy``, ``orderBy``, ``distinct``, ``repartition``) triggers a full shuffle stage.  When multiple such operations are chained without materialising the intermediate result (``cache()``/``checkpoint()``), Spark must re-execute the entire preceding lineage if any executor fails mid-way through the sequence, and the query plan grows deeply nested, making debugging difficult.

Even without failures, each shuffle amplifies spill risk: shuffle data from stage 1 is immediately read and shuffled again in stage 2 without any opportunity to filter or compact it first.

Consider whether filters or projections can be pushed between the shuffles to reduce data volume, and whether intermediate results should be checkpointed to truncate the lineage.
findings.recommendation: Insert ``df.cache()`` or ``df.checkpoint()`` between shuffle operations to materialise the intermediate result and truncate the lineage. Also check whether a ``filter()`` can be applied between the shuffles to reduce the data volume entering the second shuffle.
findings.before_code:
df2 = df.groupBy('a').agg(sum('b'))
df3 = df2.join(lookup, 'a')
df4 = df3.orderBy('b')
findings.after_code:
df2 = df.groupBy('a').agg(sum('b')).cache()
df3 = df2.join(lookup, 'a')
df4 = df3.orderBy('b')
findings.estimated_impact: Compounded shuffle I/O; full lineage re-execution on any task failure

findings.message: Python UDF(s) detected (2 found) but spark.python.worker.memory is not set (default 512 MB may be insufficient)
findings.explanation: Each Python UDF invocation launches a separate Python worker subprocess per executor task slot. Each worker's memory is bounded by ``spark.python.worker.memory`` (default 512 MB). When UDFs process large pandas DataFrames or load ML models, workers easily exhaust their allocation and are killed, causing tasks to fail with a confusing ``WorkerLostFailureReason``. Explicitly setting this config documents the intent and prevents accidental resource exhaustion during data volume growth.
findings.recommendation: Add ``.config("spark.python.worker.memory", "1g")`` (adjust based on UDF memory footprint). For pandas UDFs processing wide DataFrames, 2 GB or more may be needed.
findings.estimated_impact: Python worker OOM when UDFs process large inputs; silent task failures

findings.message: @udf function 'normalise_email' executes row-at-a-time — consider a native Spark function or @pandas_udf
findings.explanation:
A Python UDF registered with ``@udf`` (or ``udf(lambda ...)``) is called once per row.  For every row Spark must:

1. Serialise the row from JVM objects to Python pickle format.
2. Deserialise in the Python worker process.
3. Execute the Python function.
4. Serialise the result back to JVM format.

This per-row round-trip typically adds 2–10× overhead compared to using native Spark SQL functions.  Worse, the JVM cannot apply predicate pushdown, column pruning, or whole-stage code generation across a UDF boundary.

Alternatives in order of preference:

• **Native Spark SQL functions** (``from pyspark.sql.functions import *``):   cover most common transformations.
• **``@pandas_udf``**: processes a full partition as a ``pd.Series``/  ``pd.DataFrame``, batch-serialising rows instead of one at a time.
• **SQL expressions via ``expr()``**: leverage Catalyst optimisation.
findings.recommendation: Replace with a native Spark function (e.g., ``lower(col('x'))``). If business logic requires Python, use ``@pandas_udf`` for vectorised batch processing instead of row-at-a-time execution.
findings.after_code:
# Native SQL function — no serialisation overhead
df.withColumn('normalised', lower(trim(col('s'))))
findings.estimated_impact: 2–10× slowdown per row; blocks Catalyst code-generation pipeline

findings.message: @udf 'normalise_email' blocks Catalyst predicate pushdown — replace with a native SQL function to restore optimisation
findings.explanation:
Catalyst's predicate pushdown optimisation moves ``filter()``/``where()`` expressions as close to the data source as possible — ideally into the reader itself (Parquet/ORC column pruning, partition elimination).

A ``@udf`` is a black-box from Catalyst's perspective: it cannot inspect the Python function body, so it cannot safely push filters past it.  Any ``filter()`` applied to a column produced by a UDF must execute *after* the UDF has run on every row.  This prevents:

• Parquet/ORC predicate pushdown (physical plan skips row groups early).
• Partition pruning at the file reader level.
• Whole-stage code generation across the UDF boundary.

Replacing the UDF with a native Spark SQL expression restores all of these optimisations because Catalyst can then inspect and reorder the predicate freely.
findings.recommendation: Replace the @udf with a native Spark SQL expression to restore predicate pushdown. If Python logic is unavoidable, use @pandas_udf which has a narrower optimisation boundary.
findings.after_code:
# Native functions — Catalyst can push filter to the source
df.filter(trim(lower(col('s'))) == 'target')
findings.estimated_impact: Full table scan before filter; no partition/row-group elimination

findings.recommendation: Replace the @udf with a native Spark SQL expression to restore predicate pushdown. If Python logic is unavoidable, use @pandas_udf which has a narrower optimisation boundary.
findings.after_code:
# Native functions — Catalyst can push filter to the source
df.filter(trim(lower(col('s'))) == 'target')
findings.estimated_impact: Full table scan before filter; no partition/row-group elimination

findings.message: 3 shuffle operations in sequence without caching: groupBy, join, groupBy
findings.explanation:
Each wide transformation (``join``, ``groupBy``, ``orderBy``, ``distinct``, ``repartition``) triggers a full shuffle stage.  When multiple such operations are chained without materialising the intermediate result (``cache()``/``checkpoint()``), Spark must re-execute the entire preceding lineage if any executor fails mid-way through the sequence, and the query plan grows deeply nested, making debugging difficult.

Even without failures, each shuffle amplifies spill risk: shuffle data from stage 1 is immediately read and shuffled again in stage 2 without any opportunity to filter or compact it first.

Consider whether filters or projections can be pushed between the shuffles to reduce data volume, and whether intermediate results should be checkpointed to truncate the lineage.
findings.recommendation: Insert ``df.cache()`` or ``df.checkpoint()`` between shuffle operations to materialise the intermediate result and truncate the lineage. Also check whether a ``filter()`` can be applied between the shuffles to reduce the data volume entering the second shuffle.
findings.before_code:
df2 = df.groupBy('a').agg(sum('b'))
df3 = df2.join(lookup, 'a')
df4 = df3.orderBy('b')
findings.after_code:
df2 = df.groupBy('a').agg(sum('b')).cache()
df3 = df2.join(lookup, 'a')
df4 = df3.orderBy('b')
findings.estimated_impact: Compounded shuffle I/O; full lineage re-execution on any task failure

findings.message: join() without broadcast() hint — wrap small DataFrames in broadcast()
findings.explanation:
When one side of a join is significantly smaller than the other (a typical fact-dimension or fact-lookup pattern), wrapping it in ``broadcast()`` tells Spark to send the small table to every executor as a hash map, eliminating the shuffle entirely.

Without a broadcast hint, Spark falls back to a sort-merge join, which shuffles **both** DataFrames across the network — often orders of magnitude more I/O.

Spark's ``spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold`` auto-broadcasts tables below a size limit (default 10 MB).  For tables slightly above the threshold, or when auto-broadcast is disabled, explicit hints are the only way to trigger a broadcast.
findings.recommendation: Wrap the smaller DataFrame in ``broadcast()``: ``df.join(broadcast(small_df), 'id')``. For AQE-managed queries in Spark 3+, also set ``spark.sql.adaptive.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold`` to a reasonable value.
findings.estimated_impact: Eliminates shuffle on both sides; 10–100× faster for small lookups

findings.message: spark.dynamicAllocation.enabled is not true — executors held for entire job
findings.explanation:
Without dynamic allocation, Spark holds all requested executors for the entire lifetime of the application, even during idle phases (e.g., the driver is computing the next query plan). On shared clusters this wastes resources and increases queue wait times for other jobs. Dynamic allocation returns idle executors to the cluster manager, improving throughput and cost on YARN/K8s.

Note: dynamic allocation is incompatible with ``spark.streaming.dynamicAllocation`` and should not be used with Spark Structured Streaming without the shuffle service.
findings.recommendation: Add ``.config("spark.dynamicAllocation.enabled", "true")`` and also ``spark.dynamicAllocation.shuffleTracking.enabled = true`` (Spark 3+) or configure an external shuffle service.
findings.estimated_impact: Idle executor waste; reduced cluster throughput for concurrent jobs

findings.message: spark.speculation is not enabled — straggler tasks will stall the entire stage
findings.explanation:
Speculative execution detects straggler tasks (tasks running significantly slower than the median for their stage) and launches duplicate copies on other executors, using whichever finishes first. Without it, a single slow node (due to hardware degradation, noisy neighbour, or data skew) can hold up the entire stage. This is particularly valuable on heterogeneous cloud hardware where instance performance varies by 20–30%.

Note: speculation is disabled by default because it can cause duplicate side effects for non-idempotent operations (e.g., writing to an external DB). Enable it only for idempotent workloads.
findings.recommendation: Add ``.config("spark.speculation", "true")`` if your tasks are idempotent. Also set ``spark.speculation.multiplier = 1.5`` and ``spark.speculation.quantile = 0.9`` to tune the straggler detection threshold.
findings.estimated_impact: Slow nodes can stall entire stages; 2–5× job slowdown on degraded hardware

findings.message: SparkSession created without 'spark.extraListeners' or 'spark.sql.queryExecutionListeners' — register a listener to capture job metrics
findings.explanation:
Spark's listener framework (``SparkListener`` / ``QueryExecutionListener``) provides hooks for every significant event in a job's lifecycle: task start/end, stage completion, SQL query execution, shuffle read/write bytes, and more.  Without a listener, the only observability is what Spark itself logs to stdout.

Listeners can be registered two ways:

1. **Config**: ``spark.conf.set('spark.extraListeners', 'com.example.MyListener')``
2. **Programmatic**: ``spark.sparkContext.addSparkListener(MyListener())``

Common use cases:

• Emit per-job duration, shuffle bytes, and spill metrics to a metrics   backend (Datadog, Prometheus, Grafana).
• Forward SQL query events to an audit log.
• Detect slow stages and alert on-call.

Without listeners, SLA breaches are only discovered retroactively via Spark History Server — often after users have already noticed degradation.
findings.recommendation: Register a listener: spark.conf.set('spark.extraListeners', 'com.example.JobMetricsListener') or spark.sparkContext.addSparkListener(listener).
findings.estimated_impact: Blind to job performance degradation; metrics only available post-mortem

findings.message: Hardcoded read path '/data/ad_impressions' — replace with a runtime parameter or environment variable
findings.explanation:
Hardcoded paths (``s3://prod-bucket/...``, ``/data/input.parquet``) make jobs difficult to:

• **Run in multiple environments**: dev, staging, and prod point to   different buckets/paths; hardcoded values require file edits per env.
• **Test safely**: tests must not read from or write to production storage.
• **Audit**: paths embedded in code bypass infrastructure-as-code reviews.

Replace hardcoded paths with:

• **CLI arguments** via ``argparse`` or ``click``.
• **Environment variables**: ``os.environ.get('INPUT_PATH')``.
• **Config files** loaded at runtime (YAML/JSON).
• **Spark job parameters**: ``--conf spark.myapp.inputPath=...``.

This pattern separates code from configuration and is a prerequisite for environment promotion without code changes.
findings.recommendation: Replace the literal path with a parameter: input_path = args.input_path or os.environ['INPUT_PATH']. Then: spark.read.parquet(input_path).
findings.after_code:
import argparse
parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
parser.add_argument('--input-path')
args = parser.parse_args()
df = spark.read.parquet(args.input_path)
findings.estimated_impact: Environment coupling; production paths in source control; untestable

findings.message: 3 Spark action call(s) found outside any try/except block — wrap in try/except to capture executor failures
findings.explanation:
Spark actions — ``count()``, ``collect()``, write operations — submit jobs to the cluster.  Failures from executors surface as Python exceptions on the driver: ``AnalysisException``, ``SparkException``, ``IOException``, etc.  Without a ``try/except`` block, these exceptions propagate as unhandled errors and crash the driver process.

In production pipelines this means:

• **Silent failure**: the job exits with a non-zero code and downstream   jobs proceed with stale/missing data.
• **No context**: the stack trace does not capture job parameters, input   sizes, or the specific partition that failed.
• **No cleanup**: temp tables, locks, or partial writes are not rolled back.

Wrap each logical pipeline stage in a ``try/except`` and log the exception with enough context to reproduce the failure:

``except Exception as e:``
``    logger.error('stage failed: input=%s rows=%d', path, n, exc_info=True)``
``    raise``
findings.recommendation: Wrap Spark action calls in try/except Exception and log with exc_info=True before re-raising.
findings.after_code:
try:
    df = spark.read.parquet(input_path)
    result = df.join(other, 'id')
    result.write.mode('overwrite').parquet(output_path)
except Exception as e:
    logger.error('pipeline failed', exc_info=True)
    raise
findings.estimated_impact: Unhandled driver crashes; partial writes; no error context in logs

findings.message: 3 join() calls in sequence without broadcast or repartition — consider broadcasting small dimensions
findings.explanation:
In a star-schema join pattern (fact table joined to multiple dimension tables), every non-broadcast join triggers a full shuffle.  Three consecutive sort-merge joins on different keys produce three independent shuffle stages, each reading and writing the full intermediate dataset.

Strategies to reduce cost:
• **Broadcast small dimensions**: wrap any table under the broadcast threshold   in ``broadcast()`` to eliminate its shuffle entirely.
• **Repartition once by the most selective key**: if all joins are on the same   key, one upfront ``repartition('key')`` can make subsequent joins local.
• **Enable CBO** (``spark.sql.cbo.enabled = true``) so Catalyst can re-order   joins to minimise intermediate result size.
• **Filter aggressively before the first join** to reduce the base row count   that flows through every subsequent join stage.
findings.recommendation: Broadcast small dimensions with ``broadcast(dim_df)``. Enable CBO to auto-reorder joins: ``spark.sql.cbo.enabled = true`` and ``spark.sql.statistics.histogram.enabled = true``. Repartition by the join key before the first join when all joins share a common key.
findings.estimated_impact: 3+ full shuffles; each shuffle writes/reads entire intermediate dataset

findings.message: left join() without null handling — add fillna() or dropna() to prevent silent null propagation
findings.explanation:
A left join always produces ``null`` values for right-side columns when no matching row exists.  If those nulls are not handled explicitly, they silently propagate through every subsequent aggregation, filter, and write operation.

Common failure modes:
• ``count('column')`` returns fewer rows than ``count('*')`` due to null exclusion
• ``sum('amount')`` returns a wrong total because nulls are skipped
• ``filter(col > 0)`` silently drops all unmatched rows
• Writing to Parquet with ``NOT NULL`` schema constraints fails at write time

Handling nulls explicitly (``fillna`` / ``dropna`` / ``isNotNull`` filter / ``coalesce``) makes the intent clear and prevents downstream data quality issues.
findings.recommendation: After a left join, handle nulls explicitly: ``df.join(other, 'id', 'left').fillna(0, subset=['amount'])`` or ``df.join(other, 'id', 'left').dropna(subset=['required_col'])``.
findings.estimated_impact: Silent null propagation; wrong aggregation results and data quality bugs

findings.message: left join() without null handling — add fillna() or dropna() to prevent silent null propagation
findings.explanation:

findings.message: File has 3 join(s) but 'spark.sql.cbo.joinReorder.enabled' is not 'true' — enable join reordering with CBO
findings.explanation:
With ``spark.sql.cbo.joinReorder.enabled = true`` and CBO enabled, Catalyst uses a dynamic-programming algorithm (DP-based bushy join enumeration) to find the lowest-cost ordering of N-way joins.  The optimal order can reduce the sizes of intermediate DataFrames dramatically — e.g., joining a small lookup table first shrinks the result before the expensive join.

Without join reordering, Catalyst executes joins in the left-to-right order written in the source, which may produce large intermediate results that make subsequent joins slow or trigger out-of-memory errors.

Requirements: ``spark.sql.cbo.enabled = true`` must also be set, and table statistics must be collected via ``ANALYZE TABLE``.
findings.recommendation: Enable join reordering: spark.conf.set('spark.sql.cbo.joinReorder.enabled', 'true'). Also ensure spark.sql.cbo.enabled is true and stats are collected.
findings.after_code:
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.cbo.enabled', 'true')
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.cbo.joinReorder.enabled', 'true')
# Catalyst will reorder to process small tables first
result = a.join(huge, 'id').join(b, 'key').join(small, 'ref')
findings.estimated_impact: Sub-optimal join order; large intermediate DataFrames; possible OOM

findings.message: File has 3 join(s) but no ANALYZE TABLE statement found — collect statistics to improve CBO estimates
findings.explanation:
Spark's Cost-Based Optimiser and runtime adaptive strategies (AQE) rely on per-table and per-column statistics to make accurate estimates.  These statistics are populated by running ``ANALYZE TABLE t COMPUTE STATISTICS`` (or the column-level variant) before the query.

Without statistics:

• Join strategy selection defaults to heuristics (size thresholds).
• AQE skew detection uses partition byte counts rather than row counts.
• CBO's cardinality estimates are off, leading to poor join ordering.

Run ``ANALYZE TABLE`` once after data is written, or schedule it as part of the pipeline's post-write step.  For Delta tables, statistics are maintained automatically when ``delta.dataSkippingNumIndexedCols`` is set.
findings.recommendation: Add: spark.sql('ANALYZE TABLE my_table COMPUTE STATISTICS FOR ALL COLUMNS') before the join, or run it as a post-write step in the pipeline.
findings.after_code:
spark.sql('ANALYZE TABLE events COMPUTE STATISTICS FOR ALL COLUMNS')
spark.sql('ANALYZE TABLE users COMPUTE STATISTICS FOR ALL COLUMNS')
result = spark.table('events').join(spark.table('users'), 'user_id')
findings.estimated_impact: CBO operates without accurate stats; poor join strategies; missed optimisations

findings.message: write to parquet without partitionBy() — add partition columns for query pruning
findings.explanation:
Writing analytics data (Parquet, ORC, Delta) without a ``partitionBy`` clause places all output in a single directory.  Readers that filter on common columns (date, region, status) must scan every file rather than pruning to a relevant subset.

Partition pruning is one of the highest-impact Spark optimisations: partitioning a 10 TB table by ``date`` and filtering on a single day reduces the scan from 10 TB to ~27 GB (assuming 3 years of data).

Good partition columns are:
• **Low-cardinality** (hundreds to low thousands of distinct values):   date, year, month, region, status, event_type.
• **Aligned with query filters**: the most common ``WHERE`` clause columns.
• **Not too granular**: ``date`` is good; ``timestamp`` creates millions   of tiny files.
findings.recommendation: Add ``partitionBy('date', 'region')`` (or your natural query dimensions) before the write terminal. Choose low-cardinality columns that align with how the data is queried.
findings.estimated_impact: Full table scans on every query; no partition pruning possible

findings.message: Parquet write without explicit compression codec — set option('compression', 'snappy') or 'zstd'
findings.explanation:
Parquet's default compression codec depends on the Spark version and cluster configuration (``spark.sql.parquet.compression.codec``).  In older versions the default was ``uncompressed``; in Spark 2.0+ it is ``snappy``.  Relying on the default makes job behaviour cluster-dependent.

Recommendations by use case:

• **Snappy**: fast compression/decompression, moderate ratio — good default   for hot data accessed frequently.
• **Zstd**: better ratio than snappy with comparable speed — prefer for cold   data or when storage cost matters.
• **Gzip/brotli**: best ratio but slower — use for archival.

Set it explicitly on the write call so the codec is visible in code review.
findings.recommendation: Add a compression option: ``df.write.option('compression', 'snappy').parquet('path')`` or ``df.write.option('compression', 'zstd').parquet('path')``.
findings.estimated_impact: Cluster-dependent codec; may write uncompressed on some environments

findings.explanation:
Columns like ``user_id``, ``customer_id``, and ``product_id`` often follow power-law distributions in production: a handful of 'whale' users generate millions of events, and a handful of popular products receive most orders.  When joining a fact table on such a column, the reducer tasks for those popular IDs receive far more rows than average, causing straggler tasks.

The classic remedy is *salting*: append a random bucket number (0–N) to the join key so that one popular ID is distributed across N tasks instead of one:

2. Explode the smaller table into N copies, each tagged with a salt value.

N = 10

findings.message: File has 5 join(s) but 'spark.sql.cbo.joinReorder.enabled' is not 'true' — enable join reordering with CBO
findings.explanation:
With ``spark.sql.cbo.joinReorder.enabled = true`` and CBO enabled, Catalyst uses a dynamic-programming algorithm (DP-based bushy join enumeration) to find the lowest-cost ordering of N-way joins.  The optimal order can reduce the sizes of intermediate DataFrames dramatically — e.g., joining a small lookup table first shrinks the result before the expensive join.

Without join reordering, Catalyst executes joins in the left-to-right order written in the source, which may produce large intermediate results that make subsequent joins slow or trigger out-of-memory errors.

Requirements: ``spark.sql.cbo.enabled = true`` must also be set, and table statistics must be collected via ``ANALYZE TABLE``.
findings.recommendation: Enable join reordering: spark.conf.set('spark.sql.cbo.joinReorder.enabled', 'true'). Also ensure spark.sql.cbo.enabled is true and stats are collected.
findings.after_code:
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.cbo.enabled', 'true')
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.cbo.joinReorder.enabled', 'true')
# Catalyst will reorder to process small tables first
result = a.join(huge, 'id').join(b, 'key').join(small, 'ref')
findings.estimated_impact: Sub-optimal join order; large intermediate DataFrames; possible OOM

findings.message: File has 5 join(s) but no ANALYZE TABLE statement found — collect statistics to improve CBO estimates
findings.explanation:
Spark's Cost-Based Optimiser and runtime adaptive strategies (AQE) rely on per-table and per-column statistics to make accurate estimates.  These statistics are populated by running ``ANALYZE TABLE t COMPUTE STATISTICS`` (or the column-level variant) before the query.

Without statistics:

• Join strategy selection defaults to heuristics (size thresholds).
• AQE skew detection uses partition byte counts rather than row counts.
• CBO's cardinality estimates are off, leading to poor join ordering.

Run ``ANALYZE TABLE`` once after data is written, or schedule it as part of the pipeline's post-write step.  For Delta tables, statistics are maintained automatically when ``delta.dataSkippingNumIndexedCols`` is set.
findings.recommendation: Add: spark.sql('ANALYZE TABLE my_table COMPUTE STATISTICS FOR ALL COLUMNS') before the join, or run it as a post-write step in the pipeline.
findings.after_code:
spark.sql('ANALYZE TABLE events COMPUTE STATISTICS FOR ALL COLUMNS')
spark.sql('ANALYZE TABLE users COMPUTE STATISTICS FOR ALL COLUMNS')
result = spark.table('events').join(spark.table('users'), 'user_id')
findings.estimated_impact: CBO operates without accurate stats; poor join strategies; missed optimisations

findings.message: 6 Spark action call(s) found outside any try/except block — wrap in try/except to capture executor failures
findings.explanation:
Spark actions — ``count()``, ``collect()``, write operations — submit jobs to the cluster.  Failures from executors surface as Python exceptions on the driver: ``AnalysisException``, ``SparkException``, ``IOException``, etc.  Without a ``try/except`` block, these exceptions propagate as unhandled errors and crash the driver process.

In production pipelines this means:

• **Silent failure**: the job exits with a non-zero code and downstream   jobs proceed with stale/missing data.
• **No context**: the stack trace does not capture job parameters, input   sizes, or the specific partition that failed.
• **No cleanup**: temp tables, locks, or partial writes are not rolled back.

Wrap each logical pipeline stage in a ``try/except`` and log the exception with enough context to reproduce the failure:

``except Exception as e:``
``    logger.error('stage failed: input=%s rows=%d', path, n, exc_info=True)``
``    raise``
findings.recommendation: Wrap Spark action calls in try/except Exception and log with exc_info=True before re-raising.
findings.after_code:
try:
    df = spark.read.parquet(input_path)
    result = df.join(other, 'id')
    result.write.mode('overwrite').parquet(output_path)
except Exception as e:
    logger.error('pipeline failed', exc_info=True)
    raise
findings.estimated_impact: Unhandled driver crashes; partial writes; no error context in logs

findings.message: 3 join() calls in sequence without broadcast or repartition — consider broadcasting small dimensions
findings.explanation:
In a star-schema join pattern (fact table joined to multiple dimension tables), every non-broadcast join triggers a full shuffle.  Three consecutive sort-merge joins on different keys produce three independent shuffle stages, each reading and writing the full intermediate dataset.

Strategies to reduce cost:
• **Broadcast small dimensions**: wrap any table under the broadcast threshold   in ``broadcast()`` to eliminate its shuffle entirely.
• **Repartition once by the most selective key**: if all joins are on the same   key, one upfront ``repartition('key')`` can make subsequent joins local.
• **Enable CBO** (``spark.sql.cbo.enabled = true``) so Catalyst can re-order   joins to minimise intermediate result size.
• **Filter aggressively before the first join** to reduce the base row count   that flows through every subsequent join stage.
findings.recommendation: Broadcast small dimensions with ``broadcast(dim_df)``. Enable CBO to auto-reorder joins: ``spark.sql.cbo.enabled = true`` and ``spark.sql.statistics.histogram.enabled = true``. Repartition by the join key before the first join when all joins share a common key.
findings.estimated_impact: 3+ full shuffles; each shuffle writes/reads entire intermediate dataset

findings.message: left join() without null handling — add fillna() or dropna() to prevent silent null propagation
findings.explanation:
A left join always produces ``null`` values for right-side columns when no matching row exists.  If those nulls are not handled explicitly, they silently propagate through every subsequent aggregation, filter, and write operation.

Common failure modes:
• ``count('column')`` returns fewer rows than ``count('*')`` due to null exclusion
• ``sum('amount')`` returns a wrong total because nulls are skipped
• ``filter(col > 0)`` silently drops all unmatched rows
• Writing to Parquet with ``NOT NULL`` schema constraints fails at write time

Handling nulls explicitly (``fillna`` / ``dropna`` / ``isNotNull`` filter / ``coalesce``) makes the intent clear and prevents downstream data quality issues.
findings.recommendation: After a left join, handle nulls explicitly: ``df.join(other, 'id', 'left').fillna(0, subset=['amount'])`` or ``df.join(other, 'id', 'left').dropna(subset=['required_col'])``.
findings.estimated_impact: Silent null propagation; wrong aggregation results and data quality bugs

findings.message: write to parquet without partitionBy() — add partition columns for query pruning
findings.explanation:
Writing analytics data (Parquet, ORC, Delta) without a ``partitionBy`` clause places all output in a single directory.  Readers that filter on common columns (date, region, status) must scan every file rather than pruning to a relevant subset.

Partition pruning is one of the highest-impact Spark optimisations: partitioning a 10 TB table by ``date`` and filtering on a single day reduces the scan from 10 TB to ~27 GB (assuming 3 years of data).

Good partition columns are:
• **Low-cardinality** (hundreds to low thousands of distinct values):   date, year, month, region, status, event_type.
• **Aligned with query filters**: the most common ``WHERE`` clause columns.
• **Not too granular**: ``date`` is good; ``timestamp`` creates millions   of tiny files.
findings.recommendation: Add ``partitionBy('date', 'region')`` (or your natural query dimensions) before the write terminal. Choose low-cardinality columns that align with how the data is queried.
findings.estimated_impact: Full table scans on every query; no partition pruning possible

findings.message: Parquet write without explicit compression codec — set option('compression', 'snappy') or 'zstd'
findings.explanation:
Parquet's default compression codec depends on the Spark version and cluster configuration (``spark.sql.parquet.compression.codec``).  In older versions the default was ``uncompressed``; in Spark 2.0+ it is ``snappy``.  Relying on the default makes job behaviour cluster-dependent.

Recommendations by use case:

• **Snappy**: fast compression/decompression, moderate ratio — good default   for hot data accessed frequently.
• **Zstd**: better ratio than snappy with comparable speed — prefer for cold   data or when storage cost matters.
• **Gzip/brotli**: best ratio but slower — use for archival.

Set it explicitly on the write call so the codec is visible in code review.
findings.recommendation: Add a compression option: ``df.write.option('compression', 'snappy').parquet('path')`` or ``df.write.option('compression', 'zstd').parquet('path')``.
findings.estimated_impact: Cluster-dependent codec; may write uncompressed on some environments

findings.message: File has 2 write operation(s) but no Python logging — add logger.info() calls to record row counts and timing
findings.explanation:
Multi-stage Spark jobs that write results in several steps benefit greatly from intermediate logging: recording row counts, elapsed time, and key metrics after each stage makes it trivial to pinpoint where a slow or incorrect run diverged from expectation.

Without logging:

• A job that runs for 3 hours and produces wrong output requires re-running   with added instrumentation to diagnose.
• Silent data drops (a filter removes all rows) are invisible.
• Capacity planning is guesswork without recorded row counts over time.

Recommended pattern:

``n = df.count()``
``logger.info('stage enriched: %d rows', n)``

Use Python's built-in ``logging`` module rather than ``print()`` so that log level, timestamps, and destination can be controlled at runtime.
findings.recommendation: Add import logging; logger = logging.getLogger(__name__) and log row counts after each major transformation: logger.info('step X: %d rows', df.count()).
findings.after_code:
import logging
logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

enriched = raw.join(meta, 'id').withColumn('score', compute(col('v')))
logger.info('enriched rows: %d', enriched.count())
enriched.write.mode('overwrite').parquet('s3://bucket/enriched')
summary = enriched.groupBy('cat').agg(count('*'))
logger.info('summary rows: %d', summary.count())
summary.write.mode('overwrite').parquet('s3://bucket/summary')
findings.estimated_impact: Silent data drops and slow stages invisible without post-mortem log analysis

findings.message: spark.dynamicAllocation.enabled is not true — executors held for entire job
findings.explanation:
Without dynamic allocation, Spark holds all requested executors for the entire lifetime of the application, even during idle phases (e.g., the driver is computing the next query plan). On shared clusters this wastes resources and increases queue wait times for other jobs. Dynamic allocation returns idle executors to the cluster manager, improving throughput and cost on YARN/K8s.

Note: dynamic allocation is incompatible with ``spark.streaming.dynamicAllocation`` and should not be used with Spark Structured Streaming without the shuffle service.
findings.recommendation: Add ``.config("spark.dynamicAllocation.enabled", "true")`` and also ``spark.dynamicAllocation.shuffleTracking.enabled = true`` (Spark 3+) or configure an external shuffle service.
findings.estimated_impact: Idle executor waste; reduced cluster throughput for concurrent jobs

findings.message: spark.speculation is not enabled — straggler tasks will stall the entire stage
findings.explanation:
Speculative execution detects straggler tasks (tasks running significantly slower than the median for their stage) and launches duplicate copies on other executors, using whichever finishes first. Without it, a single slow node (due to hardware degradation, noisy neighbour, or data skew) can hold up the entire stage. This is particularly valuable on heterogeneous cloud hardware where instance performance varies by 20–30%.

Note: speculation is disabled by default because it can cause duplicate side effects for non-idempotent operations (e.g., writing to an external DB). Enable it only for idempotent workloads.
findings.recommendation: Add ``.config("spark.speculation", "true")`` if your tasks are idempotent. Also set ``spark.speculation.multiplier = 1.5`` and ``spark.speculation.quantile = 0.9`` to tune the straggler detection threshold.
findings.estimated_impact: Slow nodes can stall entire stages; 2–5× job slowdown on degraded hardware

findings.message: SparkSession created without 'spark.extraListeners' or 'spark.sql.queryExecutionListeners' — register a listener to capture job metrics
findings.explanation:
Spark's listener framework (``SparkListener`` / ``QueryExecutionListener``) provides hooks for every significant event in a job's lifecycle: task start/end, stage completion, SQL query execution, shuffle read/write bytes, and more.  Without a listener, the only observability is what Spark itself logs to stdout.

Listeners can be registered two ways:

1. **Config**: ``spark.conf.set('spark.extraListeners', 'com.example.MyListener')``
2. **Programmatic**: ``spark.sparkContext.addSparkListener(MyListener())``

Common use cases:

• Emit per-job duration, shuffle bytes, and spill metrics to a metrics   backend (Datadog, Prometheus, Grafana).
• Forward SQL query events to an audit log.
• Detect slow stages and alert on-call.

Without listeners, SLA breaches are only discovered retroactively via Spark History Server — often after users have already noticed degradation.
findings.recommendation: Register a listener: spark.conf.set('spark.extraListeners', 'com.example.JobMetricsListener') or spark.sparkContext.addSparkListener(listener).
findings.estimated_impact: Blind to job performance degradation; metrics only available post-mortem

findings.message: File has 1 join(s) but no ANALYZE TABLE statement found — collect statistics to improve CBO estimates
findings.explanation:
Spark's Cost-Based Optimiser and runtime adaptive strategies (AQE) rely on per-table and per-column statistics to make accurate estimates.  These statistics are populated by running ``ANALYZE TABLE t COMPUTE STATISTICS`` (or the column-level variant) before the query.

Without statistics:

• Join strategy selection defaults to heuristics (size thresholds).
• AQE skew detection uses partition byte counts rather than row counts.
• CBO's cardinality estimates are off, leading to poor join ordering.

Run ``ANALYZE TABLE`` once after data is written, or schedule it as part of the pipeline's post-write step.  For Delta tables, statistics are maintained automatically when ``delta.dataSkippingNumIndexedCols`` is set.
findings.recommendation: Add: spark.sql('ANALYZE TABLE my_table COMPUTE STATISTICS FOR ALL COLUMNS') before the join, or run it as a post-write step in the pipeline.
findings.after_code:
spark.sql('ANALYZE TABLE events COMPUTE STATISTICS FOR ALL COLUMNS')
spark.sql('ANALYZE TABLE users COMPUTE STATISTICS FOR ALL COLUMNS')
result = spark.table('events').join(spark.table('users'), 'user_id')
findings.estimated_impact: CBO operates without accurate stats; poor join strategies; missed optimisations

findings.message: 3 Spark action call(s) found outside any try/except block — wrap in try/except to capture executor failures
findings.explanation:
Spark actions — ``count()``, ``collect()``, write operations — submit jobs to the cluster.  Failures from executors surface as Python exceptions on the driver: ``AnalysisException``, ``SparkException``, ``IOException``, etc.  Without a ``try/except`` block, these exceptions propagate as unhandled errors and crash the driver process.

In production pipelines this means:

• **Silent failure**: the job exits with a non-zero code and downstream   jobs proceed with stale/missing data.
• **No context**: the stack trace does not capture job parameters, input   sizes, or the specific partition that failed.
• **No cleanup**: temp tables, locks, or partial writes are not rolled back.

Wrap each logical pipeline stage in a ``try/except`` and log the exception with enough context to reproduce the failure:

``except Exception as e:``
``    logger.error('stage failed: input=%s rows=%d', path, n, exc_info=True)``
``    raise``
findings.recommendation: Wrap Spark action calls in try/except Exception and log with exc_info=True before re-raising.
findings.after_code:
try:
    df = spark.read.parquet(input_path)
    result = df.join(other, 'id')
    result.write.mode('overwrite').parquet(output_path)
except Exception as e:
    logger.error('pipeline failed', exc_info=True)
    raise
findings.estimated_impact: Unhandled driver crashes; partial writes; no error context in logs

findings.message: write to parquet without partitionBy() — add partition columns for query pruning
findings.explanation:
Writing analytics data (Parquet, ORC, Delta) without a ``partitionBy`` clause places all output in a single directory.  Readers that filter on common columns (date, region, status) must scan every file rather than pruning to a relevant subset.

Partition pruning is one of the highest-impact Spark optimisations: partitioning a 10 TB table by ``date`` and filtering on a single day reduces the scan from 10 TB to ~27 GB (assuming 3 years of data).

Good partition columns are:
• **Low-cardinality** (hundreds to low thousands of distinct values):   date, year, month, region, status, event_type.
• **Aligned with query filters**: the most common ``WHERE`` clause columns.
• **Not too granular**: ``date`` is good; ``timestamp`` creates millions   of tiny files.
findings.recommendation: Add ``partitionBy('date', 'region')`` (or your natural query dimensions) before the write terminal. Choose low-cardinality columns that align with how the data is queried.
findings.estimated_impact: Full table scans on every query; no partition pruning possible

findings.message: Parquet write without explicit compression codec — set option('compression', 'snappy') or 'zstd'
findings.explanation:
Parquet's default compression codec depends on the Spark version and cluster configuration (``spark.sql.parquet.compression.codec``).  In older versions the default was ``uncompressed``; in Spark 2.0+ it is ``snappy``.  Relying on the default makes job behaviour cluster-dependent.

Recommendations by use case:

• **Snappy**: fast compression/decompression, moderate ratio — good default   for hot data accessed frequently.
• **Zstd**: better ratio than snappy with comparable speed — prefer for cold   data or when storage cost matters.
• **Gzip/brotli**: best ratio but slower — use for archival.

Set it explicitly on the write call so the codec is visible in code review.
findings.recommendation: Add a compression option: ``df.write.option('compression', 'snappy').parquet('path')`` or ``df.write.option('compression', 'zstd').parquet('path')``.
findings.estimated_impact: Cluster-dependent codec; may write uncompressed on some environments

findings.message: File has 2 write operation(s) but no Python logging — add logger.info() calls to record row counts and timing
findings.explanation:
Multi-stage Spark jobs that write results in several steps benefit greatly from intermediate logging: recording row counts, elapsed time, and key metrics after each stage makes it trivial to pinpoint where a slow or incorrect run diverged from expectation.

Without logging:

• A job that runs for 3 hours and produces wrong output requires re-running   with added instrumentation to diagnose.
• Silent data drops (a filter removes all rows) are invisible.
• Capacity planning is guesswork without recorded row counts over time.

Recommended pattern:

``n = df.count()``
``logger.info('stage enriched: %d rows', n)``

Use Python's built-in ``logging`` module rather than ``print()`` so that log level, timestamps, and destination can be controlled at runtime.
findings.recommendation: Add import logging; logger = logging.getLogger(__name__) and log row counts after each major transformation: logger.info('step X: %d rows', df.count()).
findings.after_code:
import logging
logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

enriched = raw.join(meta, 'id').withColumn('score', compute(col('v')))
logger.info('enriched rows: %d', enriched.count())
enriched.write.mode('overwrite').parquet('s3://bucket/enriched')
summary = enriched.groupBy('cat').agg(count('*'))
logger.info('summary rows: %d', summary.count())
summary.write.mode('overwrite').parquet('s3://bucket/summary')
findings.estimated_impact: Silent data drops and slow stages invisible without post-mortem log analysis

findings.message: spark.dynamicAllocation.enabled is not true — executors held for entire job
findings.explanation:
Without dynamic allocation, Spark holds all requested executors for the entire lifetime of the application, even during idle phases (e.g., the driver is computing the next query plan). On shared clusters this wastes resources and increases queue wait times for other jobs. Dynamic allocation returns idle executors to the cluster manager, improving throughput and cost on YARN/K8s.

Note: dynamic allocation is incompatible with ``spark.streaming.dynamicAllocation`` and should not be used with Spark Structured Streaming without the shuffle service.
findings.recommendation: Add ``.config("spark.dynamicAllocation.enabled", "true")`` and also ``spark.dynamicAllocation.shuffleTracking.enabled = true`` (Spark 3+) or configure an external shuffle service.
findings.estimated_impact: Idle executor waste; reduced cluster throughput for concurrent jobs

findings.message: spark.speculation is not enabled — straggler tasks will stall the entire stage
findings.explanation:
Speculative execution detects straggler tasks (tasks running significantly slower than the median for their stage) and launches duplicate copies on other executors, using whichever finishes first. Without it, a single slow node (due to hardware degradation, noisy neighbour, or data skew) can hold up the entire stage. This is particularly valuable on heterogeneous cloud hardware where instance performance varies by 20–30%.

Note: speculation is disabled by default because it can cause duplicate side effects for non-idempotent operations (e.g., writing to an external DB). Enable it only for idempotent workloads.
findings.recommendation: Add ``.config("spark.speculation", "true")`` if your tasks are idempotent. Also set ``spark.speculation.multiplier = 1.5`` and ``spark.speculation.quantile = 0.9`` to tune the straggler detection threshold.
findings.estimated_impact: Slow nodes can stall entire stages; 2–5× job slowdown on degraded hardware

findings.message: SparkSession created without 'spark.extraListeners' or 'spark.sql.queryExecutionListeners' — register a listener to capture job metrics
findings.explanation:
Spark's listener framework (``SparkListener`` / ``QueryExecutionListener``) provides hooks for every significant event in a job's lifecycle: task start/end, stage completion, SQL query execution, shuffle read/write bytes, and more.  Without a listener, the only observability is what Spark itself logs to stdout.

Listeners can be registered two ways:

1. **Config**: ``spark.conf.set('spark.extraListeners', 'com.example.MyListener')``
2. **Programmatic**: ``spark.sparkContext.addSparkListener(MyListener())``

Common use cases:

• Emit per-job duration, shuffle bytes, and spill metrics to a metrics   backend (Datadog, Prometheus, Grafana).
• Forward SQL query events to an audit log.
• Detect slow stages and alert on-call.

Without listeners, SLA breaches are only discovered retroactively via Spark History Server — often after users have already noticed degradation.
findings.recommendation: Register a listener: spark.conf.set('spark.extraListeners', 'com.example.JobMetricsListener') or spark.sparkContext.addSparkListener(listener).
findings.estimated_impact: Blind to job performance degradation; metrics only available post-mortem

findings.message: 3 join() calls in sequence without broadcast or repartition — consider broadcasting small dimensions
findings.explanation:
In a star-schema join pattern (fact table joined to multiple dimension tables), every non-broadcast join triggers a full shuffle.  Three consecutive sort-merge joins on different keys produce three independent shuffle stages, each reading and writing the full intermediate dataset.

Strategies to reduce cost:
• **Broadcast small dimensions**: wrap any table under the broadcast threshold   in ``broadcast()`` to eliminate its shuffle entirely.
• **Repartition once by the most selective key**: if all joins are on the same   key, one upfront ``repartition('key')`` can make subsequent joins local.
• **Enable CBO** (``spark.sql.cbo.enabled = true``) so Catalyst can re-order   joins to minimise intermediate result size.
• **Filter aggressively before the first join** to reduce the base row count   that flows through every subsequent join stage.
findings.recommendation: Broadcast small dimensions with ``broadcast(dim_df)``. Enable CBO to auto-reorder joins: ``spark.sql.cbo.enabled = true`` and ``spark.sql.statistics.histogram.enabled = true``. Repartition by the join key before the first join when all joins share a common key.
findings.estimated_impact: 3+ full shuffles; each shuffle writes/reads entire intermediate dataset

findings.message: left join() without null handling — add fillna() or dropna() to prevent silent null propagation
findings.explanation:
A left join always produces ``null`` values for right-side columns when no matching row exists.  If those nulls are not handled explicitly, they silently propagate through every subsequent aggregation, filter, and write operation.

Common failure modes:
• ``count('column')`` returns fewer rows than ``count('*')`` due to null exclusion
• ``sum('amount')`` returns a wrong total because nulls are skipped
• ``filter(col > 0)`` silently drops all unmatched rows
• Writing to Parquet with ``NOT NULL`` schema constraints fails at write time

Handling nulls explicitly (``fillna`` / ``dropna`` / ``isNotNull`` filter / ``coalesce``) makes the intent clear and prevents downstream data quality issues.
findings.recommendation: After a left join, handle nulls explicitly: ``df.join(other, 'id', 'left').fillna(0, subset=['amount'])`` or ``df.join(other, 'id', 'left').dropna(subset=['required_col'])``.
findings.estimated_impact: Silent null propagation; wrong aggregation results and data quality bugs

findings.message: join() on 'account_id' may skew in production — enable AQE skew join or implement salting
findings.explanation:
Columns like ``user_id``, ``customer_id``, and ``product_id`` often follow power-law distributions in production: a handful of 'whale' users generate millions of events, and a handful of popular products receive most orders.  When joining a fact table on such a column, the reducer tasks for those popular IDs receive far more rows than average, causing straggler tasks.

The classic remedy is *salting*: append a random bucket number (0–N) to the join key so that one popular ID is distributed across N tasks instead of one:

1. Add ``salt = (rand() * N).cast('int')`` to the larger table.
2. Explode the smaller table into N copies, each tagged with a salt value.
3. Join on ``(original_key, salt)`` instead of ``original_key`` alone.

AQE's skew join optimisation (``spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabled = true``) handles many cases automatically in Spark 3+ and should be the first option.
findings.recommendation: Enable AQE skew join first: ``spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabled = true``. If skew persists, implement salting: add ``(rand()*N).cast('int')`` as a salt column and join on ``(key, salt)``.
findings.before_code: df.join(events, 'user_id')  # user_id may be highly skewed
findings.after_code:
# Salted join
N = 10
df_salted = df.withColumn('salt', (rand()*N).cast('int'))
events_salted = events.withColumn('salt', explode(array([lit(i) for i in range(N)])))
df_salted.join(events_salted, ['user_id', 'salt'])
findings.estimated_impact: 'Whale' keys cause one reducer to handle O(N) more rows than average

findings.message: File has 3 join(s) but 'spark.sql.cbo.joinReorder.enabled' is not 'true' — enable join reordering with CBO
findings.explanation:
With ``spark.sql.cbo.joinReorder.enabled = true`` and CBO enabled, Catalyst uses a dynamic-programming algorithm (DP-based bushy join enumeration) to find the lowest-cost ordering of N-way joins.  The optimal order can reduce the sizes of intermediate DataFrames dramatically — e.g., joining a small lookup table first shrinks the result before the expensive join.

Without join reordering, Catalyst executes joins in the left-to-right order written in the source, which may produce large intermediate results that make subsequent joins slow or trigger out-of-memory errors.

Requirements: ``spark.sql.cbo.enabled = true`` must also be set, and table statistics must be collected via ``ANALYZE TABLE``.
findings.recommendation: Enable join reordering: spark.conf.set('spark.sql.cbo.joinReorder.enabled', 'true'). Also ensure spark.sql.cbo.enabled is true and stats are collected.
findings.after_code:
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.cbo.enabled', 'true')
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.cbo.joinReorder.enabled', 'true')
# Catalyst will reorder to process small tables first
result = a.join(huge, 'id').join(b, 'key').join(small, 'ref')
findings.estimated_impact: Sub-optimal join order; large intermediate DataFrames; possible OOM

findings.message: Parquet write without explicit compression codec — set option('compression', 'snappy') or 'zstd'
findings.explanation:
Parquet's default compression codec depends on the Spark version and cluster configuration (``spark.sql.parquet.compression.codec``).  In older versions the default was ``uncompressed``; in Spark 2.0+ it is ``snappy``.  Relying on the default makes job behaviour cluster-dependent.

Recommendations by use case:

• **Snappy**: fast compression/decompression, moderate ratio — good default   for hot data accessed frequently.
• **Zstd**: better ratio than snappy with comparable speed — prefer for cold   data or when storage cost matters.
• **Gzip/brotli**: best ratio but slower — use for archival.

Set it explicitly on the write call so the codec is visible in code review.
findings.recommendation: Add a compression option: ``df.write.option('compression', 'snappy').parquet('path')`` or ``df.write.option('compression', 'zstd').parquet('path')``.
findings.estimated_impact: Cluster-dependent codec; may write uncompressed on some environments

findings.message: File has 2 write operation(s) but no Python logging — add logger.info() calls to record row counts and timing
findings.explanation:
Multi-stage Spark jobs that write results in several steps benefit greatly from intermediate logging: recording row counts, elapsed time, and key metrics after each stage makes it trivial to pinpoint where a slow or incorrect run diverged from expectation.

Without logging:

• A job that runs for 3 hours and produces wrong output requires re-running   with added instrumentation to diagnose.
• Silent data drops (a filter removes all rows) are invisible.
• Capacity planning is guesswork without recorded row counts over time.

Recommended pattern:

``n = df.count()``
``logger.info('stage enriched: %d rows', n)``

Use Python's built-in ``logging`` module rather than ``print()`` so that log level, timestamps, and destination can be controlled at runtime.
findings.recommendation: Add import logging; logger = logging.getLogger(__name__) and log row counts after each major transformation: logger.info('step X: %d rows', df.count()).
findings.after_code:
import logging
logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

enriched = raw.join(meta, 'id').withColumn('score', compute(col('v')))
logger.info('enriched rows: %d', enriched.count())
enriched.write.mode('overwrite').parquet('s3://bucket/enriched')
summary = enriched.groupBy('cat').agg(count('*'))
logger.info('summary rows: %d', summary.count())
summary.write.mode('overwrite').parquet('s3://bucket/summary')
findings.estimated_impact: Silent data drops and slow stages invisible without post-mortem log analysis

findings.message: 3 Spark action call(s) found outside any try/except block — wrap in try/except to capture executor failures
findings.explanation:
Spark actions — ``count()``, ``collect()``, write operations — submit jobs to the cluster.  Failures from executors surface as Python exceptions on the driver: ``AnalysisException``, ``SparkException``, ``IOException``, etc.  Without a ``try/except`` block, these exceptions propagate as unhandled errors and crash the driver process.

In production pipelines this means:

• **Silent failure**: the job exits with a non-zero code and downstream   jobs proceed with stale/missing data.
• **No context**: the stack trace does not capture job parameters, input   sizes, or the specific partition that failed.
• **No cleanup**: temp tables, locks, or partial writes are not rolled back.

Wrap each logical pipeline stage in a ``try/except`` and log the exception with enough context to reproduce the failure:

``except Exception as e:``
``    logger.error('stage failed: input=%s rows=%d', path, n, exc_info=True)``
``    raise``
findings.recommendation: Wrap Spark action calls in try/except Exception and log with exc_info=True before re-raising.
findings.after_code:
try:
    df = spark.read.parquet(input_path)
    result = df.join(other, 'id')
    result.write.mode('overwrite').parquet(output_path)
except Exception as e:
    logger.error('pipeline failed', exc_info=True)
    raise
findings.estimated_impact: Unhandled driver crashes; partial writes; no error context in logs

findings.message: Hardcoded write path '/output/fraud_high_risk' — replace with a runtime parameter or environment variable
findings.explanation:
Hardcoded paths (``s3://prod-bucket/...``, ``/data/input.parquet``) make jobs difficult to:

• **Run in multiple environments**: dev, staging, and prod point to   different buckets/paths; hardcoded values require file edits per env.
• **Test safely**: tests must not read from or write to production storage.
• **Audit**: paths embedded in code bypass infrastructure-as-code reviews.

Replace hardcoded paths with:

• **CLI arguments** via ``argparse`` or ``click``.
• **Environment variables**: ``os.environ.get('INPUT_PATH')``.
• **Config files** loaded at runtime (YAML/JSON).
• **Spark job parameters**: ``--conf spark.myapp.inputPath=...``.

This pattern separates code from configuration and is a prerequisite for environment promotion without code changes.
findings.recommendation: Replace the literal path with a parameter: input_path = args.input_path or os.environ['INPUT_PATH']. Then: spark.read.parquet(input_path).
findings.after_code:
import argparse
parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
parser.add_argument('--input-path')
args = parser.parse_args()
df = spark.read.parquet(args.input_path)
findings.estimated_impact: Environment coupling; production paths in source control; untestable

findings.message: write to parquet without partitionBy() — add partition columns for query pruning
findings.explanation:
Writing analytics data (Parquet, ORC, Delta) without a ``partitionBy`` clause places all output in a single directory.  Readers that filter on common columns (date, region, status) must scan every file rather than pruning to a relevant subset.

Partition pruning is one of the highest-impact Spark optimisations: partitioning a 10 TB table by ``date`` and filtering on a single day reduces the scan from 10 TB to ~27 GB (assuming 3 years of data).

Good partition columns are:
• **Low-cardinality** (hundreds to low thousands of distinct values):   date, year, month, region, status, event_type.
• **Aligned with query filters**: the most common ``WHERE`` clause columns.
• **Not too granular**: ``date`` is good; ``timestamp`` creates millions   of tiny files.
findings.recommendation: Add ``partitionBy('date', 'region')`` (or your natural query dimensions) before the write terminal. Choose low-cardinality columns that align with how the data is queried.
findings.estimated_impact: Full table scans on every query; no partition pruning possible

findings.message: spark.dynamicAllocation.enabled is not true — executors held for entire job
findings.explanation:
Without dynamic allocation, Spark holds all requested executors for the entire lifetime of the application, even during idle phases (e.g., the driver is computing the next query plan). On shared clusters this wastes resources and increases queue wait times for other jobs. Dynamic allocation returns idle executors to the cluster manager, improving throughput and cost on YARN/K8s.

Note: dynamic allocation is incompatible with ``spark.streaming.dynamicAllocation`` and should not be used with Spark Structured Streaming without the shuffle service.
findings.recommendation: Add ``.config("spark.dynamicAllocation.enabled", "true")`` and also ``spark.dynamicAllocation.shuffleTracking.enabled = true`` (Spark 3+) or configure an external shuffle service.
findings.estimated_impact: Idle executor waste; reduced cluster throughput for concurrent jobs

findings.message: spark.speculation is not enabled — straggler tasks will stall the entire stage
findings.explanation:
Speculative execution detects straggler tasks (tasks running significantly slower than the median for their stage) and launches duplicate copies on other executors, using whichever finishes first. Without it, a single slow node (due to hardware degradation, noisy neighbour, or data skew) can hold up the entire stage. This is particularly valuable on heterogeneous cloud hardware where instance performance varies by 20–30%.

Note: speculation is disabled by default because it can cause duplicate side effects for non-idempotent operations (e.g., writing to an external DB). Enable it only for idempotent workloads.
findings.recommendation: Add ``.config("spark.speculation", "true")`` if your tasks are idempotent. Also set ``spark.speculation.multiplier = 1.5`` and ``spark.speculation.quantile = 0.9`` to tune the straggler detection threshold.
findings.estimated_impact: Slow nodes can stall entire stages; 2–5× job slowdown on degraded hardware

findings.message: SparkSession created without 'spark.extraListeners' or 'spark.sql.queryExecutionListeners' — register a listener to capture job metrics
findings.explanation:
Spark's listener framework (``SparkListener`` / ``QueryExecutionListener``) provides hooks for every significant event in a job's lifecycle: task start/end, stage completion, SQL query execution, shuffle read/write bytes, and more.  Without a listener, the only observability is what Spark itself logs to stdout.

Listeners can be registered two ways:

1. **Config**: ``spark.conf.set('spark.extraListeners', 'com.example.MyListener')``
2. **Programmatic**: ``spark.sparkContext.addSparkListener(MyListener())``

Common use cases:

• Emit per-job duration, shuffle bytes, and spill metrics to a metrics   backend (Datadog, Prometheus, Grafana).
• Forward SQL query events to an audit log.
• Detect slow stages and alert on-call.

Without listeners, SLA breaches are only discovered retroactively via Spark History Server — often after users have already noticed degradation.
findings.recommendation: Register a listener: spark.conf.set('spark.extraListeners', 'com.example.JobMetricsListener') or spark.sparkContext.addSparkListener(listener).
findings.estimated_impact: Blind to job performance degradation; metrics only available post-mortem

findings.message: left join() without null handling — add fillna() or dropna() to prevent silent null propagation
findings.explanation:
A left join always produces ``null`` values for right-side columns when no matching row exists.  If those nulls are not handled explicitly, they silently propagate through every subsequent aggregation, filter, and write operation.

Common failure modes:
• ``count('column')`` returns fewer rows than ``count('*')`` due to null exclusion
• ``sum('amount')`` returns a wrong total because nulls are skipped
• ``filter(col > 0)`` silently drops all unmatched rows
• Writing to Parquet with ``NOT NULL`` schema constraints fails at write time

Handling nulls explicitly (``fillna`` / ``dropna`` / ``isNotNull`` filter / ``coalesce``) makes the intent clear and prevents downstream data quality issues.
findings.recommendation: After a left join, handle nulls explicitly: ``df.join(other, 'id', 'left').fillna(0, subset=['amount'])`` or ``df.join(other, 'id', 'left').dropna(subset=['required_col'])``.
findings.estimated_impact: Silent null propagation; wrong aggregation results and data quality bugs

findings.message: join() on 'session_id' may skew in production — enable AQE skew join or implement salting
findings.explanation:
Columns like ``user_id``, ``customer_id``, and ``product_id`` often follow power-law distributions in production: a handful of 'whale' users generate millions of events, and a handful of popular products receive most orders.  When joining a fact table on such a column, the reducer tasks for those popular IDs receive far more rows than average, causing straggler tasks.

The classic remedy is *salting*: append a random bucket number (0–N) to the join key so that one popular ID is distributed across N tasks instead of one:

1. Add ``salt = (rand() * N).cast('int')`` to the larger table.
2. Explode the smaller table into N copies, each tagged with a salt value.
3. Join on ``(original_key, salt)`` instead of ``original_key`` alone.

AQE's skew join optimisation (``spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabled = true``) handles many cases automatically in Spark 3+ and should be the first option.
findings.recommendation: Enable AQE skew join first: ``spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabled = true``. If skew persists, implement salting: add ``(rand()*N).cast('int')`` as a salt column and join on ``(key, salt)``.
findings.before_code: df.join(events, 'user_id')  # user_id may be highly skewed
findings.after_code:
# Salted join
N = 10
df_salted = df.withColumn('salt', (rand()*N).cast('int'))
events_salted = events.withColumn('salt', explode(array([lit(i) for i in range(N)])))
df_salted.join(events_salted, ['user_id', 'salt'])
findings.estimated_impact: 'Whale' keys cause one reducer to handle O(N) more rows than average

findings.message: filter() applied after join() — push the filter before join() to reduce shuffle volume
findings.explanation:
Applying a filter *after* a join is correct but suboptimal: the join shuffles and processes all rows from both sides before the filter discards some of them.  If the filter is based on a column from one of the join inputs (not a computed column), applying it *before* the join reduces the shuffle data volume proportionally.

Example: ``df.join(other, 'id').filter('status = "active"')`` — if ``status`` is a column in ``df``, filtering ``df`` to active rows before the join sends fewer rows through the shuffle.

Catalyst's predicate pushdown optimiser handles this automatically for simple cases, but UDF-based filters and complex expressions are opaque to Catalyst and will not be pushed down.
findings.recommendation: Apply filter()/where() to each join input before the join: ``df.filter('status = "active"').join(other.filter(...), 'id')``.
findings.estimated_impact: Shuffle includes rows that will be discarded; wasted network I/O

findings.message: File has 2 join(s) but no ANALYZE TABLE statement found — collect statistics to improve CBO estimates
findings.explanation:
Spark's Cost-Based Optimiser and runtime adaptive strategies (AQE) rely on per-table and per-column statistics to make accurate estimates.  These statistics are populated by running ``ANALYZE TABLE t COMPUTE STATISTICS`` (or the column-level variant) before the query.

Without statistics:

• Join strategy selection defaults to heuristics (size thresholds).
• AQE skew detection uses partition byte counts rather than row counts.
• CBO's cardinality estimates are off, leading to poor join ordering.

Run ``ANALYZE TABLE`` once after data is written, or schedule it as part of the pipeline's post-write step.  For Delta tables, statistics are maintained automatically when ``delta.dataSkippingNumIndexedCols`` is set.
findings.recommendation: Add: spark.sql('ANALYZE TABLE my_table COMPUTE STATISTICS FOR ALL COLUMNS') before the join, or run it as a post-write step in the pipeline.
findings.after_code:
spark.sql('ANALYZE TABLE events COMPUTE STATISTICS FOR ALL COLUMNS')
spark.sql('ANALYZE TABLE users COMPUTE STATISTICS FOR ALL COLUMNS')
result = spark.table('events').join(spark.table('users'), 'user_id')
findings.estimated_impact: CBO operates without accurate stats; poor join strategies; missed optimisations
